coredata.dc:title: Preparation and study of CDs-WO3 composites with enhanced photocatalytic antimicrobial properties and degradation of dyes
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Qing
Wen, Guanbin
Yang, Zhengfang
Guo, Qianqian
Zhang, Bin
Nie, Yanli
Wang, Dongquan
coredata.dc:description:
In this study, carbon dots (CDs) and tungsten trioxide (WO3) were combined to prepare CDs-WO3 composites for photocatalytic treatment of bacteria and organic dyes in wastewater. The optical properties, antimicrobial properties, antimicrobial mechanism, and photocatalytic degradation performance were also investigated. The CDs-WO3 composites exhibited enhanced UV absorption intensity, broadened visible light absorption range, good charge transfer and carrier separation abilities, and reduced bandgap. The antimicrobial rate of CDs-WO3 against Staphylococcus aureus can reach 98 %. The antibacterial mechanism of CDs-WO3 was found to be the reactive oxygen species damage mechanism. Electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) analysis revealed that the reactive oxygen species signal intensity of CDs-WO3 was significantly higher than that of WO3, indicating that more ·O2
- and ·OH were produced. The analysis of bacterial cell activity demonstrated that CDs-WO3 composites reduced the activity of respiratory chain dehydrogenase in bacteria, which resulted in increased lipid peroxidation in the cell membrane. The photocatalytic degradation performance showed that the photocatalytic degradation efficiency of CDs-WO3 for methylene blue and malachite green reached 87 % and 88.04 %, respectively. The application of CDs-WO3 in real water bodies also showed good antibacterial effect and dye degradation.
coredata.dcterms:subject: CDs-WO3
Photocatalysis
Antibacterial
Methylene blue
Malachite green

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Photocatalytic degradation of cationic dye MB
Photocatalytic degradation of the anionic dye MG

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Major Science and Technology Projects in Yunnan Province

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) SEM (200 nm); (b)-(f) SEM (25 µm) element mapping images of CDs-WO3; (g) TEM images and size distribution of CDs; (h) EDS analysis of CDs-WO3 composites.
XRD pattern of CDs-WO3 (a); FTIR pattern (b); XPS full spectrum (c); C1s high-resolution XPS pattern (d); O1s high-resolution XPS pattern (e); W4f high-resolution XPS pattern (f).
UV–VIS diffuse reflectance spectra (a); PL spectra (b); bandgap values: CDs (c); WO3 (d); CDs-WO3 (e).
Antimicrobial experiments of WO3 and CDs-WO3 (a), antimicrobial rate histogram (b).

Analysis of antimicrobial mechanism, glutathione (GSH) shielding agent antimicrobial experiment (a); histogram of antimicrobial rate of glutathione (GSH) shielding agent antimicrobial experiment (b); histogram of zeta potential test (c); antimicrobial experiment with different reactive oxygen species (ROS) shielding agents (d); histogram of antimicrobial rate of different reactive oxygen species (ROS) shielding agent antimicrobial experiment (e).

EPR characterization analysis, ·OH detection under dark condition: WO3 (a); CDs-WO3 (e), ·OH detection under light condition: WO3 (b); CDs-WO3 (f), ·O2
- detection under dark condition: WO3 (c); CDs-WO3 (g); and ·O2
- detection under light condition: WO3 (d); CDs-WO3 (h).
Analysis of the metabolic activity of bacterial cells, activity of respiratory chain dehydrogenase of bacteria (a); analysis of the degree of lipid peroxidation of bacteria (b).
Bacterial morphology analysis, S. aureus (a); CDs-WO3 in contact with S. aureus (b).
UV absorption curves of MB under CDs (a); WO3 (b); CDs- WO3 (c); photocatalytic degradation efficiency (d).
UV absorption curves of MG under CDs (a); WO3 (b); CDs- WO3 (c); photocatalytic degradation efficiency (d).
Zero order kinetics: MB (a), MG (d); first order kinetics: MB (b), MG (e); second order kinetics: MB (c), MG (f).

head.ce:title: Preparation and study of CDs-WO3 composites with enhanced photocatalytic antimicrobial properties and degradation of dyes

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Yunnan forestry and grassland technology extension station, Yunnan, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Yunnan forestry and grassland technology extension station

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Yunnan forestry and grassland technology extension station, Yunnan, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Zhenkang Miaoling Jinyuan Agricultural Technology Co., LTD., Yunnan, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Zhenkang Miaoling Jinyuan Agricultural Technology Co., LTD.

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Zhenkang Miaoling Jinyuan Agricultural Technology Co., LTD.,Yunnan, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para:
In this study, carbon dots (CDs) and tungsten trioxide (WO3) were combined to prepare CDs-WO3 composites for photocatalytic treatment of bacteria and organic dyes in wastewater. The optical properties, antimicrobial properties, antimicrobial mechanism, and photocatalytic degradation performance were also investigated. The CDs-WO3 composites exhibited enhanced UV absorption intensity, broadened visible light absorption range, good charge transfer and carrier separation abilities, and reduced bandgap. The antimicrobial rate of CDs-WO3 against Staphylococcus aureus can reach 98 %. The antibacterial mechanism of CDs-WO3 was found to be the reactive oxygen species damage mechanism. Electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) analysis revealed that the reactive oxygen species signal intensity of CDs-WO3 was significantly higher than that of WO3, indicating that more ·O2
- and ·OH were produced. The analysis of bacterial cell activity demonstrated that CDs-WO3 composites reduced the activity of respiratory chain dehydrogenase in bacteria, which resulted in increased lipid peroxidation in the cell membrane. The photocatalytic degradation performance showed that the photocatalytic degradation efficiency of CDs-WO3 for methylene blue and malachite green reached 87 % and 88.04 %, respectively. The application of CDs-WO3 in real water bodies also showed good antibacterial effect and dye degradation.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Organic dyes are commonly found in wastewater from the textile, printing, and decoration industries [1–3]. Industrial wastewater often lacks disinfection due to the presence of organic compounds, some of which may contain nutrients for bacterial growth. These nutrients, when present in appropriate concentrations, can be “boosters” for bacterial growth and proliferation, exacerbating contamination in water bodies. The coexistence of organic dyes and bacteria in wastewater treatment has become a significant challenge [4–8]. Organic dyes seriously affect the appearance and transparency of water bodies, making them lose their original natural beauty. More importantly, many organic dyes are toxic and carcinogenic, accumulating in living organisms and posing a substantial threat to the health of humans and other organisms [9–11]. For example, certain organic dyes may damage human organs, trigger allergic reactions, and even induce cancer [12,13]. Similarly, the presence of bacteria, such as Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) and Salmonella, can trigger a variety of diseases in humans and animals, including symptoms like diarrhea, vomiting, and fever, which may even be life-threatening in severe cases [14,15]. Therefore, organic dyes and bacteria in water bodies pose great harm to humans, organisms, and the environment. It is necessary to improve wastewater treatment methods to reduce the release of organic dyes and bacteria to protect water resources and ecological environment.

Organic dyes, with complex chemical structures, are resistant to degradation and usually contain toxic compounds. Additionally, pathogenic bacteria in water sources pose a major threat to the environment and human health, turning water bodies into vectors for disease transmission [16]. Therefore, the development of wastewater treatment materials that can effectively treat these contaminants in water holds considerable promise for practical applications [17]. Such materials can significantly improve the efficiency and effectiveness of wastewater treatment, reducing pollutant levels to meet stricter release standards. Moreover, they help protect the ecological environment. Without effective treatment, organic dyes and bacteria in water bodies may cause serious harm to soil and organisms. Effective treatment can reduce their damage to the ecosystem, maintain ecological balance, and safeguard the health and stability of the ecological environment. Furthermore, by combining the treatment of organic dyes and bacteria, the process can be optimized to reduce energy consumption and the use of chemical usage, thus ensuring resource efficiency and cost-effectiveness.

Photocatalytic treatment is an effective strategy for the purification of various water pollutants [18,19], which is characterized by mild reaction conditions, low energy consumption, high degradation efficiencies for various pollutants, and ease of operation [20]. Tungsten trioxide (WO3) is recognized as one of the most efficient photocatalysts for the purification of various water pollutants due to its stable physicochemical properties, low toxicity, relatively narrow bandgap (2.4 −2.8 eV), and high oxidizing ability of valence band holes [21]. However, the photocatalytic performance of pure WO3 is limited by a few factors. First, its intrinsic bandgap energy (2.4 −2.8 eV) severely limits its solar energy utilization efficiency [22]. Second, its high surface charge carrier complexation rate and slow participation in interfacial redox reactions lead to its relatively low photocatalytic efficiency [23]. Therefore, the preparation of composites with a wide range of light absorption and a high rate of electron-hole separation is considered as a potential strategy to improve the photocatalytic efficiency. CDs, a new member of the carbon family with particle sizes smaller than 10 nm [24–27], have attracted significant attention for their unique two-photon absorption cross-section, upconversion ability, low toxicity, high biocompatibility, and excellent electron transfer and electron pooling properties [28–32]. Therefore, CDs can be used as co-catalysts to establish surface separation centers for electrons and reduce the rate of carrier complexation [33].

In this study, an efficient binary photocatalytic composite, CDs-WO3, was prepared by compositing CDs with WO3. This composite reduces the intrinsic bandgap energy of WO3, extends its light absorption range, and improves its electron-hole separation efficiency. The biomass-derived CDs not only facilitate the recycling of waste biomass resources but also promote the multifunctional response of the composite in water treatment processes. The CDs-WO3 composites demonstrated effective photocatalytic degradation of the cationic dye methylene blue (MB) and the anionic dye malachite green (MG), while also exhibiting effective destruction of bacterial microorganisms. Unlike most previous studies, which focus on photocatalysts with single-function response, the present study highlights a multifunctional response, which provides a certain theoretical basis and experimental foundation for multifunctional response water body treatment materials.

The shells of macadamia nuts were washed and dried at 100 °C for 12 h, and then pulverized into fine powder. 0.3 g of brown macadamia nut shell powder was dispersed in 80 mL of distilled water in a beaker, stirred to make the dispersion homogeneous, and then subjected to ultrasonic treatment in an ultrasonic cleaner at room temperature for 10 min. The ultrasonicated dispersion was transferred into a polytetrafluoroethylene-lined high-pressure reactor, and then subjected to a hydrothermal reaction at 200 °C for 14 h. After then naturally cooling to room temperature, the solution was filtered through a 0.1 µm aqueous microporous filter membrane to remove large particles and insoluble impurities. The resulting solution was stored at 4 °C in a refrigerator for further use.

To 40 mL of anhydrous ethanol, 0.5 g of tungsten source was added. The resulting mixture was sonicated at room temperature for 10 min, then transferred to an autoclave and maintained at 200 °C for 12 h. After completion of the reaction, the resulting homemade WO3 was collected after cooling and washing with ethanol.

To 40 mL of anhydrous ethanol, 0.5 g of tungsten source was added. Then, 40 mL of CDs were added. The resulting mixture was sonicated at room temperature for 10 min, transferred to an autoclave and maintained at 200 °C for 12 h. After completion of the reaction, the resulting CDs-WO3 composites were collected after cooling and washing with ethanol.

The photocatalytic properties of CDs-WO3 composites were evaluated using the cationic dye MB and the anionic dye MG. The suspensions were stirred continuously for approximately 30 min under dark conditions to establish the adsorption-desorption equilibrium between MB/MG and CDs-WO3. Then, a 10 W xenon lamp was turned on for photocatalysis with continuous stirring. The suspension was filtered through a pinhole filter membrane at regular intervals to remove insoluble matter, and the spectra were scanned in the wavelength range of 400 −800 nm using an ultraviolet spectrophotometer. The degradation rate of MB was determined by monitoring the change in maximum absorbance at 664 nm, while that of MG was assessed by monitoring the change in maximum absorbance at 618 nm.

The Gram-positive bacterium S. aureus (ATCC25723) was selected as the experimental strain for antibacterial testing. First, LB agar medium (tryptone 5 g, yeast powder 2.5 g, sodium chloride 5 g, distilled water 500 mL, and agar 7.5 g) was configured, poured into the plate, inverted, and set aside. Next, LB liquid medium (tryptone 5 g, yeast powder 2.5 g, sodium chloride 5 g, and distilled water 500 mL) was configured. The bacterial strains were added to an appropriate amount of sterile liquid medium, and the bacteria were cultured to create a bacterial suspension, which was diluted to 1 × 105 CFU using sterile water. CDs-WO3 composites were mixed with the diluted bacterial solution in sterile test tubes to prepare the final the suspension. All test tubes were incubated at 37 °C and 200 rpm on a shaker under light conditions for 15 min to ensure uniform interaction. After incubation, 100 μL of the upper liquid layer was aspirated and spread evenly onto the prepared LB agar plates using the spreading plate method. The plates were incubated in a constant temperature incubator for 18 −24 h. After incubation, the antibacterial rate of each sample was calculated using the colony counting method. The calculation formula was as follows:
(1)
%
( Antibacterial rate )
=
N
Control
−
N
Sample
N
Control
× %
where N Control is the number of colonies in the blank control group and N Sample is the number of colonies after the addition of sample coated plate.

The crystalline structure of CDs-WO3 was analyzed using X-ray diffraction (XRD, Rigaku Ultima IV, Japan). The microstructure of CDs was examined through field emission transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEOL JEM-2100F, Japan). The surface functional groups of CDs-WO3 were analyzed using Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, Thermo Scientific iN10, USA), while the molecular composition was determined by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Scientific K-Alpha, USA). The potential changes of bacterial cell membranes were characterized using a Zetasizer Nano ZS90 particle sizer (Malvern Panalytical, UK). A field emission scanning electron microscope (SEM, NOVANANOSEM 450) was used to characterize the microscopic morphology of CDs-WO3 and the bacterial morphology after interaction. UV–VIS diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (UV–VIS, Shimadzu UV-3600, Japan) was used to measure the light-absorption intensity of CDs-WO3 in order to calculate its bandgap. Steady-state/transient fluorescence spectrometer (PL, Edinburgh FLS1000, UK) was used to test the fluorescence intensity of CDs-WO3. Finally, Electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR, Bruker EMXplus-6/1, Germany) was used to test the reactive oxygen signal generated by CDs-WO3.

The micro-morphology and elemental distribution of CDs-WO3 were examined using SEM and EDS. Fig. 1(a) shows the SEM image of CDs-WO3, which reveals a stacked rod-like structure. Fig. 1(b-f) shows the EDS elemental mapping images of CDs-WO3, indicating that the C, O, and W elements are uniformly distributed in the CDs-WO3 composites. Fig. 1(g) presents the TEM images and size distribution images of CDs, which exhibit regular spherical particles. The particle size distribution map shows that CDs have a spherical shape with an average diameter of approximately 4.25 nm, with particle sizes ranging from 1 to 9 nm. High-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images reveal that the CDs have a good lattice spacing of 0.21 nm, which corresponds to the (100) plane of graphitic carbon [34–37]. Fig. 1(h) shows the EDS energy spectrum analysis of CDs-WO3 composite, revealing the following elemental compositions: C (5.98 %), O (17.68 %), and W (76.35 %). These results confirm the successful preparation of the composites.

In order to study the structure and composition of CDs-WO3, X-ray diffraction test was applied to analyze the crystalline phase composition of CDs-WO3. Fig. 2(a) presents the XRD patterns of WO3 and CDs-WO3. The intensity of WO3 diffraction peaks in CDs-WO3 becomes lower, probably due to the introduction of CDs, which affects its crystallinity, leading to a more dispersed arrangement and a decrease in diffraction peak intensity.

In order to investigate the surface functional groups of CDs-WO3, FTIR spectroscopy was carried out (Fig. 2(b)). The FTIR spectra of CDs show a broad peak at 3312.4 cm−1, which is attributed to the O-H stretching vibration, and a peak at 1635.9 cm−1, which is attributed to the vibrational peak of CO [33,37–39]. These results indicate that CDs have hydrophilic groups. In the CDs-WO3 composites, the peak intensities of these peaks are slightly enhanced and the peak positions are red-shifted or blue-shifted to some extent. This suggests interactions between CDs and WO3, indicating the successful formation of the composite. The molecular composition of CDs-WO3 was further determined using XPS. The XPS spectra were calibrated using the C1s standard peak and a binding energy (BE) of 284.8 eV as a reference. The full-range XPS spectra (Fig. 2(c)) confirm the presence of three typical elements, C, O, and W, in CDs-WO3, with relative contents of 34.01 %, 51.80 %, and 14.19 %, respectively. The high-resolution XPS spectra of C1s (Fig. 2(d)) show three peaks at 284.8, 286, and 289 eV, corresponding to C-C [40]/CC [39], C-O/C-N, and CO, respectively. Among these, the peak area of C-C/CC is the largest, indicating that it is mainly dominated by C-C/CC. Similarly, the high-resolution XPS spectra of O1s (Fig. 2(e)) show three peaks at 530.7 eV, 532.2 eV, and 533.2 eV, attributed to W-O, CO, and O-H, respectively, with W-O as the dominant component. W4f high resolution XPS spectrum (Fig. 2(f)) exhibits two peaks at 35.9 eV and 38 eV, attributed to W4f7/2 and W4f5/2, respectively, indicating that W6+ is the dominant valence state of W in the material [41]. The W4f peaks in CDs-WO3 composites are slightly shifted compared to the standard samples of WO3 in the literature. This indicates a change in the surface chemical environment of W due to the interaction between CDs and WO3 [33]. In conclusion, these analyses further confirm the successful preparation of CDs-WO3 composites.

Fig. 3(a) displays the UV–VIS diffuse reflectance absorption spectra, showing that the UV absorption intensity of CDs-WO3 composites is significantly enhanced and the visible absorption range is broadened compared to pure WO3. Photoluminescence spectroscopy (PL) was used to study the recombination of photogenerated carriers in the photocatalysts (Fig. 3(b)). Higher fluorescence intensity indicates a higher rate of carrier recombination. In the presence of CDs, the fluorescence peak intensity of CDs-WO3 is greatly reduced, indicating their excellent charge transfer ability and efficient carrier separation. This also indicates that CDs-WO3 composites can absorb light at larger wavelengths, thus broadening their absorption spectral range [42]. Fig. 3(c–e) shows the bandgap value of CDs, WO3, and CDs-WO3. It is obvious from the figure that the bandgap value of CDs-WO3 composites decreases compared to the others, indicating that CDs-WO3 composites have stronger absorption in the visible region [43]. In summary, these findings confirm that the prepared CDs-WO3 composites have excellent photocatalytic activity.

The antimicrobial activity of WO3 and the CDs-WO3 composites was evaluated against the Gram-positive bacterium S. aureus under both light and dark conditions. As shown in Fig. 4, the CDs-WO3 composites have the best antimicrobial effect under light conditions, achieving an antimicrobial rate of 98 %.

Fig. 5(a) illustrates the antimicrobial experiments conducted with glutathione (GSH) shielding agent, a reactive oxygen species (ROS) scavenger that shields all the ROS-induced damage. Group A is the positive control group, with only the material plus light applied. Group B is the antimicrobial experiments with both the material plus light and the GSH, and group C is the antimicrobial experiments with both the material plus light and the GSH. In group C the antimicrobial experiment was carried out by centrifuging the supernatant after the addition of GSH. In group A, there was no external intervention. In group B, GSH was added to exclude ROS damage, and in group C, there was only ion release damage. The corresponding antimicrobial mechanism is that the difference in antimicrobial rate between group A and group B represents ROS damage, while the difference between group B and group C indicates adsorption damage. The antimicrobial rate of group C only reflects ion release damage. Fig. 5(b) shows the histogram of antibacterial rate. The results confirm that the primary mechanism is ROS damage, with no evidence of adsorption or ion release damage. The surface of normal bacterial cell membrane shows negative potential, and the change in bacterial cell membrane potential can affect bacterial metabolic activity or even trigger the bacterial death. To explore the change in surface charge after the interaction between bacteria and the material, the zeta potential of CDs-WO3 before and after the contact with bacteria was tested. As shown in Fig. 5(c), the zeta potential does not shift positively after the contact between the material and the bacteria, indicating that the material does not break the negative potential of the bacterial cell membrane through electrostatic interaction. Fig. 5(d) displays the antimicrobial experiments of the materials with different kinds of ROS scavengers. Superoxide dismutase (SOD) was used as a ·O2
- scavenger, catalase (CAT) as a H2O2 scavenger, and D-mannitol (D-mannitol) as a ·OH scavenger. Combined with the histogram of antimicrobial rate in Fig. 5(e), it can be seen that SOD, CAT, and D-mannitol have different degrees of influence on the antimicrobial rate. SOD has the greatest influence, followed by CAT and then D-mannitol. These findings indicate that ·O2
- , H2O2, and ·OH all have a certain contribution to the ROS damage mechanism, with ·O2
- having the greatest effect.

The antimicrobial mechanism of CDs-WO3 was sterilized by ROS damage. In order to further verify the presence of ROS, their specific types were analyzed using EPR spectroscopy. DMPO was used as a trapping agent for superoxide anion radicals (·O2
- ) and hydroxyl radicals (·OH), forming stable compounds, DMPO-·O2
- and DMPO-·OH. Therefore, the concentrations of ·O2
- and ·OH can be represented by the intensities of their corresponding peaks [44]. As can be seen from Fig. 6(a, c, e, g), under dark conditions, very weak DMPO-·O2
- and DMPO-·OH signaling peaks are present in WO3 and CDs-WO3, indicating an almost complete absence of ·O2
- and ·OH. However, under light conditions (Fig. 6(b, d, f, h)), both samples produce obvious DMPO-·O2
- and DMPO-·OH signal peaks. The signal peaks of CDs-WO3 are more intense than those of WO3, indicating that more ·O2
- and ·OH are produced by CDs-WO3. This is attributed to the improved photocatalytic activity of CDs-WO3, which facilitates better charge transfer and higher separation efficiency of photogenerated carriers. Thus, the photocatalytic antimicrobial properties of CDs-WO3 composites were improved.

The enzyme activities of bacteria can lead to the blockage of metabolic activities and can impair bacterial viability [45]. Changes in the activity of the relevant enzymes caused by the contact with antimicrobial materials can also reflect the damage to bacteria. Iodine nitrotetrazolium chloride violet (INT), an indicator of cellular respiratory activity, is water-soluble and cleaves to the tetrazolium ring under the action of respiratory enzymes in living cells, and then it is further reduced to the fat-soluble INF, which accumulates within the cells [46]. The production of INF reflects the metabolic vigor of the cell, can be examined, and its maximum absorbance at 490 nm in bacteria. As shown in Fig. 7(a), the activity of respiratory chain dehydrogenase in the positive control group (Control (+), live bacteria) is strong, while the activity of the negative control group (Control (-), dead bacteria) shows almost no activity. In the bacteria treated with CDs-WO3 composites, the activity of respiratory chain dehydrogenase is significantly reduced compared to the Control (+).

The ROS produced by antimicrobial materials can induce lipid oxidation in bacterial cell membranes, change the permeability of cell membranes, and weaken bacterial metabolic activities. Therefore, the degree of lipid peroxidation can also reflect the degree of bacterial damage. The degree of lipid peroxidation was measured by measuring the content of malondialdehyde, a product of polyunsaturated fatty acid oxidation, in the bacterial broth after CDs-WO3 treatment [47]. Malondialdehyde has the maximum absorbance at 532 nm, allowing the degree of lipid peroxidation in bacterial cell membranes to be quantified through absorbance at 532 nm. From Fig. 7(b), it can be seen that the control group exhibits a low degree of lipid peroxidation in bacteria. In contrast, the bacterial lipid peroxidation is significantly enhanced after treatment with CDs-WO3 composites, which is consistent with the antibacterial mechanism of ROS damage. In conclusion, CDs-WO3 composites can lead to a decrease in the activity of bacterial respiratory chain dehydrogenase and significantly enhance lipid peroxidation in bacterial cell membranes, leading to bacterial inactivation or death.

In order to visually assess the damage of CDs-WO3 composites on bacteria, SEM was used to observe the morphology of bacteria before and after contact with the CDs-WO3. From Fig. 8, it can be clearly seen that the untreated bacteria exhibit an intact complete shape, full morphology, and vigorous physiological activity. In contrast, bacteria treated with CDs-WO3 show wrinkling, depression, deformation, and content leakage, ultimately leading to loss of physiological activity and bacterial death.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic degradation of cationic dye MB
ce:section.ce:para: The photocatalytic degradation performance of 10 mg of CDs-WO3 on 10 mg/L cationic dye MB was investigated. Fig. 9 displays the UV absorption spectra of the photocatalytic degradation activity. The photocatalytic degradation reaches saturation after 120 min. The photocatalytic degradation efficiency of CDs-WO3 for MB is 87 % (Fig. 9(c)), significantly higher than the 49 % efficiency achieved by WO3 alone (Fig. 9(b)). Therefore, it can be concluded that the photocatalytic degradation of MB by the CDs-WO3 composites is better.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic degradation of the anionic dye MG
ce:section.ce:para: The photocatalytic degradation performance of 10 mg of CDs-WO3 on 10 mg/L of the anionic dye MG was investigated. Fig. 10 shows the UV absorption spectra of the photocatalytic degradation activity. The photocatalytic degradation reaches saturation after 120 min. As shown in Fig. 10(b), the photocatalytic degradation efficiency of WO3 alone for MG is approximately 33.9 %, whereas the CDs-WO3 composites achieved a significantly higher efficiency of 88.04 %, as illustrated in Fig. 10(c). Therefore, it can be concluded that the CDs-WO3 composites are more effective in photocatalytic degradation of MG compared to WO3.
The resulting data were evaluated using the zero, first, and second order kinetic response models as follows [48–50]:

Zero order kinetic response model equations:
(2)
C
t
− C
0
=
k
0
t

First order kinetic response model equations:
(3)
In
C
t
C
0
=
k
1
t

Second order kinetic response model equations:
(4)
1
C
t
−
1
C
0
=
k
2
t
where C
t (mg ·L−1) and C
0 (mg ·L−1) are the reaction time t (min) and the concentration of the dye in the reaction solution at the photocatalytic onset time, respectively. k
0 is the zero order kinetic rate constant, k
1 is the first order kinetic rate constant, and k
2 is the second order kinetic rate constant.

The fitting results reveal that the first and second order kinetic reaction models are not applicable to the photocatalytic degradation process, whereas the zero order kinetic reaction model is suitable for explaining the process. For MB, the zero order kinetic reaction model ( Fig. 11(a)) shows an R2 of 0.9945, while the first order (Fig. 11(b)) and second order (Fig. 11(c)) models have R2 values of 0.9552 and 0.801, respectively. Similarly, for MG, the zero order kinetic model (Fig. 11(d)) has an R2 of 0.9768 compared to 0.9699 for the first-order model (Fig. 11(e)) and 0.8495 for the second-order model (Fig. 11(f)) R2 for MG is 0.8495. Overall, the zero order kinetic reaction model R2 > first order kinetic reaction model R2 > second order kinetic reaction model R2. These results confirm that the photocatalytic degradation processes of MB and MG by 10 mg of CDs-WO3 composites conform to the zero order kinetic reaction model.

Yunnan Dianchi water, characterized by its eutrophication and severe pollution, was selected to investigate the effectiveness of CDs-WO3 in the real water treatment applications. For antibacterial testing, untreated Yunnan Dianchi water was used as a blank control group, and Dianchi water with added CDs-WO3 was used as the experimental group. Bacteria were cultured in coated plates to observe the bacterial growth and evaluate the antibacterial effect of the material. The antimicrobial experimental effect graph is shown in Fig. S6(a). CDs-WO3 demonstrates good antimicrobial effect on the bacteria present in the real water body. To investigate photocatalytic dye degradation, MB and MG were added to the Dianchi water at equivalent concentrations to simulate real water pollutants. The experimental effect graph Fig. S6(b)) shows a degradation rate of 69.11 %. These findings indicate that CDs-WO3 performs well in treating real water bodies containing a variety of bacteria and pollutants. However, there are some shortcomings, and the effect may be reduced with the increase in bacteria and pollutant species, highlighting some limitations of the material in highly complex water environments.

In this study, multifunctional CDs-WO3 composites were prepared for wastewater treatment applications. Analysis revealed a stacked rod-like morphology, while chemical characterization confirmed the successful synthesis of the composites. Optical analysis showed enhanced UV absorption, broadened visible absorption range, good charge transfer and carrier separation, and reduced bandgap values. The antimicrobial performance is good, achieving a 98 % antibacterial rate against S. aureus under light conditions. The antibacterial mechanism was identified as ROS damage without adsorption and ion release damage. EPR test showed that the reactive oxygen signal peaks of CDs-WO3 are stronger under light conditions. CDs-WO3 can reduce the activity of bacterial respiratory chain dehydrogenase, increase lipid peroxidation of cell membrane, and induce morphology changes such as wrinkling and concavity in bacteria. CDs-WO3 also exhibited excellent photocatalytic degradation efficiency, achieving 87 % for MB and 88.04 % for MG. Moreover, practical application tests in real water treatment demonstrated a 69.11 % dye degradation rate and strong antibacterial performance, underscoring the material's potential for real-world use. This study provides a promising pathway for improving wastewater treatment efficiency while reducing costs, making it a significant contribution to environmental sustainability.

Wang Dongquan: Project administration, Funding acquisition. Zhang Bin: Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Funding acquisition. Nie Yanli: Project administration, Funding acquisition. Guo Qianqian: Supervision, Resources. Wang Qing: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Wen Guanbin: Software, Resources. Yang Zhengfang: Supervision, Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.bej.2025.109670.
